html:
Full-System Reroofing for Spartanburg County's Manufacturing Base

A reroof replaces the membrane, insulation, and where needed the deck, rather than adding another layer over a roof that has already failed once. For plants and distribution buildings along the I-85 corridor, that work gets planned around production calendars first and weather windows second.

Recover Limits Under Current Code

Building and fire code cap how many roof assemblies a structure may carry before a full tear-off is required. Many buildings in the county are already at that ceiling, particularly warehouses that picked up a recover system in the 1990s or early 2000s on top of an original built-up roof.

On the mill-era buildings scattered through downtown Spartanburg and the surrounding mill villages, the underlying deck is often wood plank or gypsum, not steel, which changes what a code official will approve for a second layer. We pull the roof's permit history before recommending a path so the scope reflects what inspectors will actually sign off on.

A recover also traps whatever moisture is already sitting in the old insulation, which shortens the life of the new membrane regardless of how well it's installed. Where a moisture survey shows wet insulation across more than a small percentage of the field, we recommend against a recover even where code would technically allow one.

Phasing Around Production Shifts

Plants supplying the automotive and tire manufacturing base near Greer and the I-85 corridor cannot shut down production to accommodate a roof crew. We break large roofs into zones sized to what can be torn off, insulated, and dried in within a single work period, so no section sits open overnight.

For facilities running multiple shifts, tear-off crews typically stage during the lowest-traffic shift and shift work areas away from active dock doors and loading lanes. Noise-sensitive operations, including any assembly or quality-control floor, get scheduled sections rather than blanket coverage.

Distribution centers along the I-85 corridor present a different constraint: continuous truck traffic. We coordinate staging areas and material hoists with the facility's logistics team so dock access stays open through the reroof.

Membrane Selection for Heat, Humidity, and Wind

Upstate South Carolina summers put sustained UV and heat load on a roof membrane, while thunderstorm season brings wind gusts capable of stressing perimeter attachment. TPO and PVC membranes handle the UV exposure with a reflective surface that reduces rooftop unit run time, which matters on buildings with dense mechanical loads.

EPDM remains a sound choice on lower-slope sections where ballast or fully-adhered systems are already engineered into the roof, and it performs well under the freeze-thaw cycling the region sees a handful of times each winter.

Wind uplift ratings matter more on the taller distribution buildings near the interstate, where corner and perimeter zones see higher pressure than the field of the roof. We size fastener patterns and adhesive coverage to the building's height and exposure category rather than defaulting to a single spec across every job.

What a Reroof Scope Typically Documents

  • Existing deck type, condition, and moisture survey results
  • Insulation thickness and R-value target for the new assembly
  • Membrane type, thickness, and attachment method
  • Perimeter edge metal and coping condition
  • Drain, scupper, and penetration flashing scope
  • Rooftop unit curb and equipment coordination

Deck Condition and Insulation on Older Buildings

Once the existing membrane and insulation come off, we document deck condition with photos before covering it back up. On the aged built-up roof assemblies common to mill-era structures, saturated insulation is the most frequent finding, and replacing it is usually the single biggest driver of energy performance improvement on the building.

Current insulation thickness on most older roofs falls well short of what's typical for new construction. A reroof is the practical point to close that gap, since the roof is already open and the added board goes in at the same labor cost as a thinner replacement.

Warranty Registration and Closeout Documentation

Manufacturer warranties on a new system are only valid if the installation is registered with the manufacturer and the crew is certified for that product line. We submit registration paperwork as part of closeout, along with as-built drawings, material submittals, and inspection photos formatted for a facility's asset records.

For REIT-owned and institutionally managed buildings, that documentation package typically needs to match a specific procurement format. We ask for the property's preferred template before closeout so the file doesn't need to be rebuilt after the fact.

What Facilities Managers Ask Before Approving a Reroof

How long does a reroof take on a large distribution building?

Timeline depends on square footage and phasing constraints more than anything else. A single-zone tear-off on a smaller office building can close out in a few days, while a multi-acre distribution roof phased around active dock operations runs several weeks.

Can crews work around night shifts and weekend production?

Yes. We build the phasing plan from the facility's shift schedule, not a standard workweek, and adjust zone sizing so no area is left open past the end of a work period.

What happens to the material that comes off the roof?

Removed membrane, insulation, and any wood blocking are hauled off site and disposed of per local waste requirements. Metal edge components are recycled where the facility has that as a preference.

Does a reroof require a new building permit?

In most jurisdictions in the county, yes. We pull the permit and coordinate the required inspections as part of the project rather than leaving that step to the facility.

How is the new insulation R-value determined?

We target current energy code minimums as a baseline, then discuss whether the facility wants to exceed that based on utility cost and rooftop unit load. The final figure goes into the submittal package for the owner's approval before material orders.
